texts.orig: Production of lithium from primary resources is lagging behind demand (12% versus 16% in 2016), cost of lithium is increasing (between 40 and 60% in 2016), battery energy density rapidly increasing versus declining cost, and estimated lithium ion battery (LIB) markets size ($77.42 billion by 2024) driven by projected demands for plug-in electric vehicles (PEVs) clearly justifies recycling. PEV technologyandprojecteddemandraiseseveralchallenges,includinglithiumdemand/scarcityandfuture technology to recover lithium from LIB waste. To address the circular economy, steady supply chain security, self-reliance, environmentsafety,environmentdirective,energysecurity,resourcesconservation,futuristiccarbonfootprint,WEEEdirectives and waste crime, recycling of LIB is an absolute essential. During the last decade, LIB recycling research and industrial recycling of LIB have attracted the interest of researchers, industrialists, and environmentalists. All have reported progress in the recovery of valuable metals like Co, but have rarely focused on lithium recovery. Hence, this paper addresses logical hypothesis and application of available technology in a fashion where lithium recycling from LIB can be addressed. ©2017Society of Chemical Industry

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Worldwide lithium production has increased by 12% whereas lithium consumptionincreasedby16%in2016.In2016thepriceof lithium carbonate increased more than 3-fold compared with previous year in China. Worldwide lithium price increased by 40-60% in 2016. 1-3 The price instability, mostly due to lithium battery supply in China, tripled in 2015, as plug-in electric vehicle (PEV) sales surged. 3 Bohlsen 3 has estimated that lithium carbonate or lithium carbonate equivalent use could reach 2430 kt just for PEV application and 2830 kt industry wide by 2025. The projected demand could account for 86% lithium share for lithium ion batteries (LIB), from the current 39%. 3 The same report also predicted the estimated total lithium carbonate equivalent production could reach 1430 kt by 2025, which is a deficit of 1400 kt (Projected lithium carbonate equivalent demand is 2830 kt versus projected production is 1430). 3 MarketWatch, Inc. has estimated the LIB market to reach $77.42 billion by 2024, based on a 12.5% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) from 2016 to 2024. 4 The technology evolution for LIB favors the higher battery energy density as well as lowers the cost for production, which favors battery energy density versus cost. US DOE global EV outlook 2016 reported PEV battery cost decrease from USD 1000 per kWh to USD 268 per kWh during 2008 to 2015, a 73% reduction in seven years. Major makers such as Tesla and GM are targeting a cost of USD 50 per kWh by 2022. The same report stated that the energy density of PEV batteries was 60 Wh L -1 in 2008 and 295 Wh L -1 in 2015, improving by almost 400%. 5 Favourable evolution of battery energy density and cost over the past decade has led to greater demands for LIBs. Considering the socio-politico-economic scenario, market competition, supply deficit, increasing price, and higher energy density versus cost, lithium supply security has become a topmost priority for LIB related technology companies in the United States, Asia-pacific, and China. 1 A UNEP report on recycling rates of metals, a status report, has indicated that less than 1% of lithium is being recycled. 6 However, researchers and economists have divided opinions on the economic need to recycle and the critical supply chain stability of lithium, but to address the circular economy, steady supply chain security, self-reliance, environment safety, environment directive, energy security, resources conservation, futuristic carbon footprint, WEEE directives and waste crime, recycling of LIB is absolutely essential. 1-4,6,7

texts.orig: and industrial processes, invariably Co and other valuable metals recovery has been the main focus. Lithium, which is considered a green energy metal, has rarely had any attention for recovery from secondaryresourceslike LIB. Lithium from LIBs is industrially recovered only by Toxco Inc. (now Retrieval Technology Inc.) and BDC Inc. using cryo-milling followed by LiOH recovery in their recycling plants in Canada and USA. 13 Considering the cost-effectiveness of the process, the technological challenge and the low levels of lithium content, recovery of lithium has received only marginal attention. The cost inefficient recovery process of lithium from LIB is the main contributing cause for poor uptake. As lithium is very reactive in air or moisture, both pyrometallurgical processes as well as cryo-processes are high energy consumption processes and have adverse carbon footprints. Hence, a cost-effective, low energy consuming, greener-recycling process needs to be developed to handle the challenges associated with next-generation lithium demand and to conserve natural resources. Hydrometallurgy may provide a solution for cost-effective recovery of lithium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This perspective article discusses the integration of suitable technologies for the cost-effective recovery of lithium from LIB recycling industry wastewater after Co recovery, where the LIB is recycled through a hydrometallurgical process as part of the process. Enrichment of lithium from wastewater by reverse osmosis (RO) followed by lithium carbonate could be a cost- effective and feasible process. Finally, integration of the proposed lithium recovery process with our earlier LIB recycling process, which has been developed for Co recovery, is discussed. 16,17,28

texts.orig: is being wasted in the waste stream and only Co is considered a valuable metal in hydrometallurgical LIB recycling. The wastewater disposed of after Co recovery in the hydrometallurgical recycling process of LIB will here be termed LIB recycling industry wastewater. This article discusses the enrichment of lithium from LIB industry wastewater by RO followed by a sequential precipitation process for purification and recovery of lithium carbonate. As explained above, because it is an energy intensive process, the pyrometallurgy process has severe limitations for lithium recovery. Instead of the pyrometallurgy process lithium can efficiently and cost-effectively be recovered by hydrometallurgy process. Figure 1 shows the industrial hydrometallurgy process for recycling LIB, with the lithium being discarded to the wastewater stream. Since lithium is present in the wastewater stream from the LIB recycling industry, in general, it could be recovered using the following stages: (i) enrichment/concentration; (ii) purification; and (iii) recovery of lithium either as elemental lithium or as a salt of lithium. Our earlier thorough investigation and review suggest that cost-effective recovery of lithium by liquid-liquid extraction is not feasible as most of the extractants like crown ether, crown ether derivatives, /u1D6FD -diketones, and /u1D6FD -diketones are either very expensive or have very limited selectivity over other monovalent cations. 29 Usually, the lithium-bearing wastewater is not free from other monovalent cations, hence, the liquid-liquid extraction of lithium using chelating extractants is not feasible from an industrial perspective, because of the high cost of extractant and selectivity of lithium over other monovalent cataions. 29 The inappropriateness of liquid-liquid extraction of lithium by acidic extractants such as Cyanex 272, D2EHPA, PC 88A, Ionquest 801 has been reported. 29 Scope for liquid-liquid extraction of lithium by only solvating extractants is very limited, with lithium extraction only possible when /u1D6FD -diketones are used as an extractant for synergism. texts.label: text
texts.orig: From a comprehensive investigation of lithium recovery in the open literature and in patents, the feasibility of cost-effective recovery of lithium from LIB recycling industry wastewater by hydrometallurgy has been hypothesized. Considering the available information in the literature, lithium recovery from LIB recycling industry wastewater could be feasible through a generalized process simplified in the flowsheet presented in Fig. 2. Lithium-bearing wastewater can be enriched cost effectively by RO or natural evaporation, similar to brine processing. Enrichment through heat-treated evaporation could be energy intensive and expensive. In the tropical environment, enrichment of lithium-bearing wastewater by natural evaporation could be an option. Lithium enrichment by a RO process could be an efficient cost-effective alternative to evaporation.

Contrary to water purification by the RO process, the rejected stream with high concentration of salt can be used for recovery. texts.orig: From a literature investigation, it can be concluded that cost-effective recovery of lithium by liquid-liquid extraction is very unlikely. The limitations are well-reflected in the literature. In fact, a few possible methods can be used for recovery of lithium by liquid-liquid extraction, i.e. LIX 51-TOPO, LIX 54-TOPO, and LIX 54-Cyanex 923 as explained by Kinugita et al. andPranolo et al., respectively. 32,33 The residual amount of divalent metal present in the wastewater may limit the liquid-liquid extraction process as commercial extractants LIX 51, LIX 54, TOPO, and Cyanex 923 have extracting ability of any divalent metal that may be present in the LIB recycling industry wastewater.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Technical feasibility of lithium enrichment by RO process (mainly the ionic rejection behavior of membrane) and cost of RO membrane are two determining factors for the techno-economic viability of the process. Typical ionic rejection behavior is depicted in Table 1, showing data adapted from Porter. 34 Salt rejection in the RO process is mainly driven by the atomic/molecular size of the impurities. Comparing the atomic size of lithium with those given in the table, then lithium enrichment by RO through an appropriate membrane appears to be a feasible option. Table 1 clearly indicates that enrichment of lithium from wastewater of LIB is quite feasible by the RO process. Current membranes have accomplished rejection rates up to 99.8%, so that 99.8% of the metallic content can be concentrated in the rejected stream of the LIB recycling industry wastewater. 35-37 Quist-Jensen et al. reported that more than 99.6% of the lithium can be enriched/recovered by an RO system from seawater where the lithium content was only 0.17 mg L -1 . 38 Numerous authors have reported efficient removal of heavy metal including Co, Ni, and Mn from wastewater by RO. 39-43 The above discussion justifies the technological feasibility of lithium recovery from LIB recycling industry wastewater.

Typical ionic rejection behavior is depicted in Table 1, showing data adapted from Porter. 34 Salt rejection in the RO process is mainly driven by the atomic/molecular size of the impurities. Comparing the atomic size of lithium with those given in the table, then lithium enrichment by RO through an appropriate membrane appears to be a feasible option. texts.orig: To understand the cost effectiveness of the lithium recovery by the RO membrane process, trends in the desalination process can be used as an example. During last three decades membrane and desalination process cost has continually decreased. 44-46 Lattemann et al. reported that desalination costs have decreased to 1$ m -3 , which is almost 5-fold less during the last quarter century. 44 According to another market report dealing with desalination in China, desalination cost is about 0.5 $ m -3 . 47 The same report indicated that water reclamation cost in china is about 0.1$ m -3 . 47 The validity of cost analysis based on comparison of seawater desalination and lithium enrichment from LIB industry wastewater by RO may be questioned or argued as speculation as both have intrinsic differences. For the RO process, between seawater and LIB industry wastewater feed, the latter is a better and less complex feed from the TDS, pH, and complex composition perspective. texts.orig: Several publications have highlighted the feasibility of economical salt/metals recovery from the concentrate/rejected stream of the desalination process. All these reports suggest that techno-economic recovery of salts from seawater is feasible, so also should be lithium recovery from LIB recycling industry wastewater. 50-53 Total salt content in the LIB recycling industry wastewater is far less than that in seawater and lithium content is much higher than in the seawater; hence, a conservative estimate suggests that the lithium enrichment cost could be almost the same or less than that of seawater desalination. RO process integration with the existing hydrometallurgical LIB process can thus address cost-effectively both wastewater treatment and lithium recovery issues.

By further managing pH around 14.00 and adding saturated solution of sodium carbonate or ammonium carbonate, lithium can be precipitated as lithium carbonate. Hence, sequential precipitation of residual divalent metals followed by lithium precipitation it quite feasible without any interference. Table 2 depicts the solubility of various lithium salt (Li 2 CO3, LiOH.H 2 O, LiCl, Li 2 SO4 ) as a function of temperature and minimum Li + content required for precipitation of lithium as their respective salt. 54 The minimum Li + content required for precipitation of lithium as salt were calculated from solubility data of the respective salts presented in Table 2. Possible precipitation of lithium carbonate can be justified from the Table 2. The same table can answer why lithium carbonate could be a cost-effective recovery process. Table 2 compares the minimum concentration required to precipitate lithium as carbonate, hydroxide, chloride, and sulfate using the respective anions. From the table, it can be clearly observed that, to precipitate out lithium as carbonate a minimum of 1.45 g L -1 and 0.68 g L -1 of Li + are required at 25 ∘ C and 100 ∘ C, respectively, which is the lowest concentration of all the lithium salts. Hence, lithium recovery as lithium carbonate by precipitation can be relatively simple and cost effective, which adds simplicity to the total recovery process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the hypothesis, theoretical analysis and literature it can reasonably be concluded that cost-effective recovery of lithium by RO enrichment followed by sequential precipitation from the wastewater of the hydrometallurgical LIB recycling process is quite feasible. Combination of two cost-effective processes, i.e. RO and lithium carbonate precipitation, for the recovery of lithium from LIB recycling industry wastewater is an environment-friendly novel process, that be integrated with existing processes to recover lithium with minimal facilities. By integrating the proposed lithium recovery process with our earlier LIB cathode material recycling process a total close loop hydrometallurgical process can be developed. The proposed process uses neither hazardous chemicals, nor expensive chemicals, hence the process can simultaneously addresssuchissuesasrecyclingLIBwithzerowastedisposal,urban mining, circular economy, and resources conservation.
